lessons.name: Why Exponents Exist
lessons.think: Write: \(2 \times 2 \times 2 \times 2\). Is there a shorter way?
ask: Why might we need a shortcut?
lessons.know: Exponents are shorthand for repeated multiplication. Example: \(2^4 = 16\).

engage.description: Fold paper repeatedly and count layers.

lessons.reflect: What surprised you about how fast the numbers grew?
lessons.evaluate: ☐ I understand ☐ I need practice ☐ I am confused
lessons.content: Exponents are shorthand for repeated multiplication.
lessons.hint: Think of exponents as repeated multiplication.

lessons.name: Laws of Exponents
ask: What happens when we combine the same base?
Do we multiply or add something?
lessons.know: Product rule: \(a^m \times a^n = a^{m+n}\). Other rules follow similar patterns.

engage.description: Use blocks to build \(2^3\) and \(2^2\), then combine them physically.

lessons.confirm: Explain why exponents are added when multiplying same bases.
lessons.reflect: Did you discover the rule or memorize it?
lessons.evaluate: ☐ I discovered the rule ☐ I memorized it ☐ I am unsure
lessons.hint: When multiplying powers with the same base, add exponents.

lessons.name: Zero & Negative Powers

engage.description: Walk forward to multiply, backward to divide.

lessons.confirm: Why is any number to the power 0 equal to 1?
lessons.reflect: How does this connect to division?
lessons.evaluate: ☐ I understand the pattern ☐ I memorized it ☐ I need help
lessons.hint: Divide by the base repeatedly until you reach exponent 0.

lessons.name: Standard Form
ask: How can we reduce large numbers?

engage.description: Students physically space digits and compress into standard form.

lessons.confirm: What makes a number valid standard form?
lessons.reflect: Where do we see such numbers in real life?
lessons.evaluate: ☐ I can convert ☐ I need practice ☐ I am confused
lessons.hint: Move the decimal until one non-zero digit remains before it.

lessons.name: Fractional Powers
ask: How are roots related to powers?
Can we rewrite roots as exponents?

engage.description: Fold paper into equal parts to represent roots.

lessons.reflect: How does this help in solving equations?
lessons.evaluate: ☐ I understand roots ☐ I need practice ☐ I am unsure

lessons.name: Exponents in Algebra
ask: What happens when a power is raised again?

engage.description: Use tiles labeled x to build powers and combine them.

lessons.confirm: Why do we multiply exponents here?
lessons.reflect: Where can this be applied in algebra?
lessons.evaluate: ☐ I can apply ☐ I need practice ☐ I am confused
lessons.hint: Multiply exponents when raising a power to another power.

lessons.name: Final Self-Assessment
lessons.know: Exponents help simplify repeated multiplication and power relationships.

engage.description: Explain one concept to a classmate.

lessons.confirm: Can you explain a rule without looking at notes?
lessons.reflect: Which area do you feel most confident in?
lessons.hint: Review each law and apply it to new examples.

worksheet.description: Generate random practice problems for Exponents & Powers.

lessons.name: Understanding Squares
lessons.think: If you multiply a number by itself, what pattern do you notice?
ask: Why is it called a square?
lessons.know: A square means multiplying a number by itself: \(a^2 = a \times a\). Example: \(5^2 = 25\).

engage.description: Find squares of numbers from 1 to 10 and observe the pattern.

lessons.reflect: Where do we see squares in real life (areas, tiles)?
lessons.evaluate: Explain why \(9^2\) is not equal to \(9 × 2\).
lessons.hint: Square means multiply the number by itself.

lessons.name: Understanding Cubes
lessons.think: What happens when you multiply a number three times?
ask: Why is it called a cube?
lessons.know: A cube means multiplying a number three times: \(a^3 = a × a × a\). Example: \(3^3 = 27\).
lessons.reflect: Where do cubes appear in real life (boxes, storage)?
lessons.evaluate: Explain why \(2^3\) is not equal to \(2 × 3\).
lessons.hint: Cube means multiply the number three times.

lessons.name: Squares vs Cubes
lessons.think: How are squares and cubes different?
lessons.know: Squares grow slower than cubes. Example: \(3^2 = 9\), \(3^3 = 27\).

engage.description: List values of \(n^2\) and \(n^3\) for n = 1 to 5.

lessons.evaluate: Explain with an example how cubes outpace squares.

real_life.title: 🏠 Area of a Square Room
real_life.story: A square room has side length 6 m.
real_life.question: What is the area?

real_life.title: 📦 Volume of a Cube Box
real_life.story: A cube box has side 4 cm.
real_life.question: What is the volume?

real_life.title: 🎲 Dice Stacking
real_life.story: You stack cubes in a 3×3×3 arrangement.
real_life.question: How many cubes are used?
